title: Venezuela Needs Regime Change: The Narrow Path to Democracy
content:
When U.S. forces swooped into Caracas to seize President Nicolás Maduro, many Venezuelans rejoiced at the dictator's ouster. The removal seemed to signal the imminent end of a regime that had long oppressed its people and sown regional instability. However, in an unusual twist for U.S. intervention, Washington removed the dictator but left the underlying dictatorship intact. Maduro’s vice president simply took over, keeping the ruling party and its deeply entrenched allies in power. In the past, U.S. military actions in Latin America typically catalyzed complete regime changes rather than partial leadership swaps.

U.S. officials have suggested that Venezuela could copy Spain’s rapid transition to democracy after the death of Francisco Franco. During Spain's transition, the regime's heirs immediately legalized opposition parties, signed power-sharing pacts, and drafted a democratic constitution. Unfortunately, the new Venezuelan regime has taken remarkably few steps to replicate that successful model. The United States and its partners seem intent on moving much more slowly, favoring incremental changes over a sudden democratic overhaul. Proponents of democracy must now hope for circumscribed reforms, such as sharing oil revenues and dismantling the coercive apparatus.

This gradual liberalization strategy mirrors Mexico's political transition during the 1990s and early 2000s. Facing a massive debt crisis, Mexico's ruling party fixed the economy through privatization and formal trade agreements before enacting minor electoral reforms. These institutional changes ultimately made it harder to manipulate elections and incentivized opposition parties to apply pressure at the ballot box. A similar opening in Venezuela could give the opposition new opportunities to push the regime for more substantial democratic reforms. However, such success requires consistent support from international actors and a genuine willingness from the regime to eventually cede power.
